current_fact.fact_text: On May 10, 1975, Sony introduced the Betamax videocassette recorder, a chunky machine that allowed people, for the first time, to easily record television broadcasts at home. Though it would eventually lose the format war to VHS, Betamax quietly changed how humans thought about time, letting shows shift from fixed broadcasts to personal archives.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

current_fact.match_reason: A pioneering home‑video format fits an episode about media nostalgia and old devices—Betamax as the VCR ancestor of the throwback MP3 player.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1967, the experimental Northrop M2‑F2 lifting‑body aircraft crashed during landing after a notoriously unstable flight, flipping and tumbling in a cloud of dust. Footage of the accident, and the idea of rebuilding a shattered pilot with technology, helped inspire the novel ‘Cyborg’ and the TV series ‘The Six Million Dollar Man’.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: A crash that inspires a man rebuilt as a cyborg connects to an episode about alter‑egos and transformed identities, blurring human and enhanced selves.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1961, Air France Flight 406 was torn apart by a bomb over the Sahara Desert, killing all 78 people on board. The wreckage fell into the vastness of the dunes, turning the world’s largest hot desert into a silent witness to one of aviation’s grim acts of sabotage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: A tragedy in the Sahara is paired with an episode rooted in desert cultures and Tuareg music, acknowledging both the harshness and resilience of life in the sands.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1997, a magnitude 7.3 earthquake struck Iran’s Khorasan Province near Qayen, killing around 1,567 people and leveling entire villages. The quake’s force rippled through adobe houses and mountain valleys, underlining how invisible stresses inside the Earth can rewrite human landscapes in a few violent seconds.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A devastating earthquake meets a reflective, hopeful episode about weathering storms and finding constancy amid upheaval.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1996, a sudden blizzard hit climbers on Mount Everest, trapping multiple expeditions high on the world’s tallest mountain. By the next day, eight people had died in thin air and whiteout conditions, in an event that would reshape how humans talk about ambition, risk, and the ethics of climbing into the death zone.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A fatal storm in the high Himalaya connects with an episode shaped by mountain landscapes and the emotional pull of high places.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1497, Amerigo Vespucci is said to have sailed from Cádiz on what some claim was his first voyage toward the lands Europeans would come to call the New World. Whether this particular journey happened as later recounted is debated, but the idea of setting off from a familiar port toward a barely imagined continent would eventually give his name to two entire hemispheres.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A voyage leaving Cádiz toward unknown western shores pairs with a seafaring, boat‑themed episode that sails musically around nearby Mediterranean coasts.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1775, a small band of colonial militiamen led by Ethan Allen and Benedict Arnold captured Fort Ticonderoga in a dawn raid, surprising the British garrison as it slept. The fort’s heavy cannons would later be dragged over winter mountains to help break the siege of Boston, turning a backwoods gambit into strategic leverage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A daring revolutionary raid against imperial power aligns with a politically charged episode centered on musical resistance and rebellion.

queue.fact_text: Also on May 10, 1775, delegates from the Thirteen Colonies convened in Philadelphia as the Second Continental Congress, improvising a wartime government while still debating loyalty to the British Crown. Within weeks they would take command of an army, and within a year they would sign a declaration that transformed protests into a revolution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

queue.match_reason: A congress wrestling with whether to break from empire mirrors an episode themed around re‑examining beliefs and daring to think again.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1796, during the Battle of Lodi, Napoleon Bonaparte forced a crossing of the Adda River by sending his troops over a narrow bridge under intense Austrian fire. The brutal, close‑quarters clash on the planks helped cement his self‑image as a man of destiny, a story he would later retell to shape his own legend.

episode.title: Sedna FM #03 - Beautiful as You Feel
episode.description: An episode about self-acceptance and inner beauty. Music that reminds us we are beautiful as we feel, embracing our authentic selves. Warm, uplifting sounds that celebrate individuality.
songs: The The - this is the day
DCTL - Deep Tannin f(eat Rafael Leafar)

queue.match_reason: A battle that Napoleon later used to construct his myth connects with an episode about how we narrate and feel our own identity and sense of self.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1688, King Narai of Ayutthaya, gravely ill, nominated his trusted official Phetracha as regent—a move that helped trigger the Siamese revolution of 1688. Within months, foreign influence was violently pushed back, the royal succession was overturned, and Phetracha himself emerged as king of a transformed kingdom.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: A pivotal power shift in the Ayutthaya Kingdom belongs beside an episode rooted in contemporary Thailand, tying past upheavals to present‑day culture and rhythm.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1933, Nazi students and officials orchestrated mass public book burnings across Germany, tossing works by Jewish, leftist, and ‘un‑German’ authors into bonfires. The flames were meant to erase ideas, but they also produced some of the 20th century’s most haunting images of cultural repression.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: An attempt to literally burn forbidden ideas pairs with a consciously political, un‑boxing episode about resisting imposed labels and speaking against oppression.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1940, during the chaos of the early Western campaign of World War II, German bombers accidentally dropped their payload on the German city of Freiburg im Breisgau. The mistaken attack killed civilians and damaged homes, a grim example of how modern war can turn inward and strike the very population it claims to protect.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A tragic, unintended bombing resonates symbolically with an episode titled “November Bomb,” linking the idea of sudden impact to a foundational, explosive mix.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 2013, construction of One World Trade Center in New York City reached a symbolic milestone when it became the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere. Rising from the site of the destroyed Twin Towers, the new spire turned steel, glass, and careful engineering into a vertical statement about memory and resilience.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A skyscraper rising from a site of loss into a symbol of renewal matches an introspective episode about healing, recovery, and looking back while moving forward.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 1503’s Las Tortugas voyage aside, another May 10 natural marvel came centuries later in the same Caribbean basin: Columbus’s naming highlighted sea turtles as ecosystem engineers. These ancient reptiles graze seagrass meadows like undersea gardeners, keeping them healthy for countless other species and quietly storing carbon in coastal waters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: Warm Caribbean waters full of turtles harmonize with a relaxed, roots‑reggae and dub selection that evokes tropical mornings and slow coastal rhythms.

queue.fact_text: On May 10, 2013’s skyscraper milestone echoes another vertical feat: the way humans turn skyward structures into cultural symbols. One World Trade Center’s height is not just a number; it’s a deliberate act of storytelling in steel, aligning architecture with memory in a city defined by its skyline.

episode.title: Morning Drops #50 - Postcards from Earth-London
episode.description:
Postcards from Earth-London is a Morning Drops walk through the city Gaia has called home for the past ten years, a place made of sound, memory, and late-night streets. 

Four tracks guide the journey: from iconic landmarks to quieter corners, from romance to reality checks, and back to the reason the city still feels alive: music.

queue.match_reason: A story about a defining skyscraper in New York pairs with an episode that treats another great city’s built environment as a map of memory and sound.

queue.fact_text: Behind May 10’s wars and voyages lies the quiet constant of the Western Hemisphere itself—a cartographic idea that divides Earth into east and west. It’s a reminder that continents and oceans existed long before borders and buildings, and that every event on this date is just one moment in a much older planetary story.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: An abstract hemispheric perspective fits an episode about travel, shifting viewpoints, and gliding across borders through world‑spanning sounds.

queue.fact_text: Threading through many of May 10’s stories is the simple act of watching the sky: from Han dynasty sunspots to modern auroras and rockets. Across millennia, humans keep looking up, turning fleeting lights, dark spots, and contrails into science, myth, technology, and music about our place under (and beyond) the stars.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: A closing reflection on sky‑watching and cosmic phenomena pairs with a ‘Cosmic’‑themed, electronic dance episode that feels like a signal from the stars.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 2024, the Sun unleashed a series of powerful eruptions that triggered the May 2024 solar storms, the strongest geomagnetic storms since the 2003 ‘Halloween storms’. Auroras spilled far beyond their usual polar haunts, painting night skies in unexpected colors and briefly reminding humans how alive and dynamic their star really is.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A cosmic, space-related event pairs with an episode built around reimagining ‘space’ and energising electronic textures.

published.fact_text: In 28 BC, during the reign of Emperor Cheng of Han, Chinese court astronomers recorded a dark blemish crossing the face of the Sun—an early dated observation of a sunspot. With no telescopes, they watched through dust and mist, turning a fleeting imperfection on the solar disk into a data point in one of the world’s oldest continuous astronomical traditions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: Ancient observers reading subtle solar shadows matches the atmospheric, contemplative mood and layered textures of trip‑hop.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1916, Ernest Shackleton’s tiny lifeboat James Caird washed up on South Georgia after an 800‑nautical‑mile voyage through the stormiest seas on Earth. The six men aboard had navigated only by dead reckoning and a few precious sextant sightings in brief breaks in the clouds, in a desperate bid to rescue the rest of their stranded Antarctic crew.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: An almost impossible open‑boat voyage belongs with an episode literally set on a sailing boat, full of sea‑bound stories and resilient moods.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1503, Christopher Columbus reached the Cayman Islands and named them Las Tortugas after the astonishing abundance of sea turtles he saw. For a brief moment, European exploration paused to take note not of gold or conquest, but of slow‑moving reptiles grazing in clear tropical waters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A discovery defined by sea turtles and clear Caribbean water pairs with an aquatic, diving‑inspired selection built around fluid, oceanic moods.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1922, the United States formally annexed Kingman Reef, a remote coral atoll in the central Pacific that has no permanent inhabitants and barely rises above sea level. It is more lagoon than land, a speck of territory ringed by coral and teeming underwater life rather than cities or roads.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A nearly invisible reef whose true richness lies underwater aligns with an episode about diving, marine life, and cinematic aquatic soundscapes.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1869, two halves of the First Transcontinental Railroad finally met at Promontory Summit, Utah Territory, where dignitaries ceremonially drove a 17.6‑karat golden spike into a polished tie. Telegraph operators wired the blows in real time, so that a nation heard, almost simultaneously, the sound of its two coasts being joined by steel.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: A continent‑spanning railway is the ultimate ‘on the move’ project, fitting an episode devoted to motion, journeys, and forward momentum.

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1946, at the White Sands Proving Ground in New Mexico, engineers launched the first V‑2 rocket under American control, sending a captured German missile arcing into the high atmosphere. These re‑purposed weapons became unwitting research tools, carrying instruments—and eventually cameras—toward the edge of space.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A rocket probing the upper atmosphere and ‘solar wind’ pairs naturally with a cinematic, electronic journey that literally opens with a track called “Solar Wind.”

published.fact_text: On May 10, 1975, Sony introduced the Betamax videocassette recorder, a chunky machine that allowed people, for the first time, to easily record television broadcasts at home. Though it would eventually lose the format war to VHS, Betamax quietly changed how humans thought about time, letting shows shift from fixed broadcasts to personal archives.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

published.match_reason: A pioneering home‑video format fits an episode about media nostalgia and old devices—Betamax as the VCR ancestor of the throwback MP3 player.
